titleStmt.title: Development of a Commentary Generation System for Western Classical Texts

keywords.term: I plan to attend the conference on-site in Lisbon

div.head: Background
div.p: The digitization of Western classical texts, exemplified by the Perseus Digital Library , has greatly expanded accessibility and opened new avenues for research. At the same time, recent efforts within the humanities have begun employing generative AI—particularly large language models (LLMs)—to produce interpretations of specific texts (Schwitzgebel et al. 2023, Truelove 2023).

We have developed Humanitext Antiqua ( https://humanitext.ai/), which employs Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG), leveraging advanced LLMs (e.g., OpenAI’s GPT) to produce reliable, context-based scholarly responses grounded in ancient Greek and Latin texts (Iwata et al. 2024a, 2024b).

div.head: Research Aim
div.p: Commentary, the interpretation of primary texts, lies at the core of classical scholarship. Humanitext Antiqua integrates primary texts, commentaries, and secondary literature into a framework, enabling broader contextual interpretations. To achieve this objective, we propose a “Commentary Generation System.”

This study presents an implementation of the system using Plato’s Republic as a case study. A central challenge in linking primary and secondary text databases is achieving correspondence between reference points. Classical works are typically arranged by volumes, chapters, sections, and lines—structures forming the basis for scholarly discourse. Strict adherence to such divisions can yield chunks too small or large, limiting context retrieval and the effective use of LLMs. Conversely, mechanically dividing texts into uniform chunks can complicate traditional referencing. Reconciling efficient chunking with established referencing practices is therefore a key focus of this research.

div.head: Methodology and Implementation

list.item:
Creation of Authority Data
Authority data was developed to link the primary text of Plato’s Republic with secondary literature, enriched with URIs. The advantages of using authority data include the ability to record additional information not found in the texts themselves and to maintain the independence of the data resource, ensuring consistency even when primary or secondary text databases are updated. The authority data includes metadata such as author, title, volume, chapter, page, and line numbers to facilitate traditional referencing. In the case of Plato, this follows the Stephanus pagination, for example, "Plato, Republic, 1, 327a."

Vectorization of Primary Texts and Secondary Literature with Metadata Linking
Using primary texts from the Perseus Digital Library, we vectorized the texts and annotated each chunk with metadata, such as text identifiers (e.g., "Plato, Republic, 1, 327a–328d"). These identifiers were linked to the corresponding authority data. Similarly, during the vectorization of secondary literature, metadata indicating references to specific passages in the primary text were extracted and linked to the authority data. This approach facilitates the integration of primary text contexts with extensive secondary literature data via authority data (Figure 1).

div.p: (Figure 1: Linking authority data with primary texts and secondary literature)

When users input queries into the system, it retrieves relevant chunks of the primary text and generates responses based on them. Simultaneously, the system utilizes authority data to retrieve related secondary literature, providing users with nuanced interpretations and contextual information. This workflow enables users to gain insights not only from the primary texts but also from the scholarly traditions and historical contexts embedded in the secondary literature. Additionally, all primary and secondary sources used in generating responses are cited and explicitly displayed, allowing users to verify the reliability of the outputs while deepening their understanding.

Currently, the system incorporates a limited selection of secondary literature that is open-source or free of copyright restrictions. Expanding the database will enable the comprehensive management of interpretative traditions spanning antiquity to the present. Additionally, the system’s multilingual design will support the integration of regional research outcomes, helping overcome linguistic barriers to achieve a more inclusive understanding of classical studies. Moving forward, preliminary usability tests and expert feedback will guide refinements in user interaction and ensure the system’s practical benefits are effectively realized.

listBibl.head: Bibliography
listBibl.bibl: Iwata, Naoya / Tanaka, Ikko / Ogawa, Jun (2024a): “Improving semantic search accuracy of classical texts through context-oriented translation”, in: Information Processing Society of Japan (ed.): Proceedings of Jinmoncon 2024 (Vol. 2024).

Iwata, Naoya / Tanaka, Ikko / Ogawa, Jun (2024b): “Utilizing large language models for research and education in Western classics”, in: Japanese Society for Artificial Intelligence (ed.): Proceedings of the 38th Annual Conference of the Japanese Society for Artificial Intelligence (Paper No. 1N4-OS-18-05).
Schwitzgebel, Eric / Schwitzgebel, Daniel / Strasser, Andrew (2023): “Creating a Large Language Model of a Philosopher”. arXiv 2302.01339 [cs.CL]. DOI: 10.48550/arXiv.2302.01339.
Truelove, K. (2023): “On the Nature of Artificial Things”. TrueSciPhi.AI, 5 December 2023. https://www.truesciphi.ai/p/on-the-nature-of-artificial-things [28.04.2025].
